difficulty.title: Choose a Challenge
difficulty.subtitle: Pick your difficulty level:
difficulty.beginnerDesc: Most cells given — learn the grid with confidence.
difficulty.easyDesc: Simple eliminations only — great for building intuition.
difficulty.mediumDesc: Requires pointing pairs and basic techniques.
difficulty.hardDesc: Naked pairs, hidden singles and more.
difficulty.masterDesc: Near-minimal clues — demands expert chains and deep logic.

hint.panelDesc: Click the Hint button during the game to activate deep logical analysis and step-by-step deconstruction of complex solving techniques.

nakedSingle.title: Naked Single
nakedSingle.step1Desc: Cell [R{row}C{col}] is surrounded by all other digits. The blue-highlighted cells in its row, column, and box block every digit except {num}. A Naked Single means only one candidate remains — visible directly without further analysis.
nakedSingle.step2Desc: Since all other digits (1–9 except {num}) are eliminated from this cell by conflicts in its row, column, and box, {num} is the only valid value.

hiddenSingleRow.title: Hidden Single — Row
hiddenSingleRow.step1Desc: Check all empty cells in row {row}. Digit {val} is not possible in any other cell of that row — blocked by their columns or boxes. Filled cells (blue) show why. This is called Hidden Single because {val} is "hidden" inside the row — not immediately obvious.
hiddenSingleRow.step2Label: Only valid placement for this digit
hiddenSingleRow.step2Desc: Since {val} can only go into cell [R{row}C{col}] within the entire row, that is the only valid placement. Enter {val}.

hiddenSingleColumn.title: Hidden Single — Column
hiddenSingleColumn.step1Desc: Check all empty cells in column {col}. Digit {val} is not possible in any other cell of that column — blocked by their rows or boxes. Filled cells (blue) show why the other positions are ruled out.
hiddenSingleColumn.step2Label: Only valid placement for this digit
hiddenSingleColumn.step2Desc: Since {val} can only go into cell [R{row}C{col}] within the entire column {col}, that is the only valid placement. Enter {val}.

hiddenSingleBox.title: Hidden Single — Box
hiddenSingleBox.step1Desc: Look at box {box} (rows {rowStart}–{rowEnd}, cols {colStart}–{colEnd}). Digit {val} is not possible in any other empty cell of that box — blocked by surrounding rows and columns. Filled cells (blue) confirm this.
hiddenSingleBox.step2Label: Only valid placement in the box
hiddenSingleBox.step2Desc: Since {val} can only go into cell [R{row}C{col}] within the entire box {box}, that is the only valid placement. Enter {val}.

nakedPairRow.title: Naked Pair — Row
nakedPairRow.step1Desc: In row {row}, the cells in columns {col1} and {col2} share exactly the same candidates [{v1}, {v2}]. A Naked Pair means these two digits must go into one of those two cells — no other digit can go there, and {v1} and {v2} cannot appear elsewhere in the row.
nakedPairRow.step2Label: Eliminate pair from the rest of the row
nakedPairRow.step2Desc: Since {v1} and {v2} are "locked" into those two cells, they cannot appear in any other cell of row {row}. Red-highlighted cells can have {v1} or {v2} removed from their candidate lists, opening new possibilities.

nakedPairColumn.title: Naked Pair — Column
nakedPairColumn.step1Label: Find the Naked Pair in the column
nakedPairColumn.step1Desc: In column {col}, the cells in rows {row1} and {row2} share exactly the same candidates [{v1}, {v2}]. Those two digits must go into one of those two cells — no other digit is possible there, and {v1} and {v2} cannot appear elsewhere in the column.
nakedPairColumn.step2Label: Eliminate pair from the rest of the column
nakedPairColumn.step2Desc: Since {v1} and {v2} are "locked" into those two cells in column {col}, they can be removed from all other cells in the same column (red). This narrows candidates and simplifies further solving.

nakedPairBox.title: Naked Pair — Box
nakedPairBox.step1Label: Find the Naked Pair in the box
nakedPairBox.step1Desc: In box {box}, cells [R{row1}C{col1}] and [R{row2}C{col2}] share the same candidates [{v1}, {v2}]. This means {v1} and {v2} must go into one of those two cells, without exception.
nakedPairBox.step2Label: Eliminate pair from the rest of the box
nakedPairBox.step2Desc: Since {v1} and {v2} are exclusively tied to those two cells in box {box}, they can be removed from all other empty cells in the same box (red). This can reveal new Naked Single or Hidden Single opportunities.

pointingPairRow.title: Pointing Pair — Row
pointingPairRow.step1Label: Spot the alignment inside the box
pointingPairRow.step1Desc: In box {box}, all remaining candidates for digit {val} lie exclusively in row {row} (blue). This means {val} must go into one of those cells — and nowhere else in that box.
pointingPairRow.step2Label: Eliminate from the rest of the row
pointingPairRow.step2Desc: Because of the alignment inside the box, {val} cannot be in any other cell of row {row} outside that box. Red cells can have {val} removed from their candidates, simplifying further solving.

pointingPairColumn.title: Pointing Pair — Column
pointingPairColumn.step1Label: Spot the alignment inside the box
pointingPairColumn.step1Desc: In box {box}, all remaining candidates for digit {val} lie exclusively in column {col} (blue). This means {val} must go into one of those cells — and nowhere else in that box.
pointingPairColumn.step2Label: Eliminate from the rest of the column
pointingPairColumn.step2Desc: Because of the alignment inside the box, {val} cannot be in any other cell of column {col} outside that box. Red cells can have {val} removed from their candidates.

nakedTriple.title: Naked Triple — {unit}
nakedTriple.step1Desc: Cells [R{row1}C{col1}], [R{row2}C{col2}] and [R{row3}C{col3}] together contain exactly three distinct candidates: {v1}, {v2}, {v3}. Each cell holds only a subset of those three digits (2 or 3). Together, this triple "locks" those digits — none of the three can go anywhere else in {unit}.
nakedTriple.step2Label: Eliminate triple from the rest of the unit
nakedTriple.step2Desc: Since {v1}, {v2} and {v3} must go into those three cells, they can be removed from all other cells in {unit} (red). This drastically narrows the possibilities.

nakedQuad.title: Naked Quad — {unit}
nakedQuad.step1Desc: Four cells in {unit} together contain exactly four candidates: {v1}, {v2}, {v3}, {v4}. Each cell holds only a subset of those four digits. This quad "locks" them — none of the four digits can go into any other cell of the same unit.
nakedQuad.step2Label: Eliminate quad from the rest of the unit
nakedQuad.step2Desc: Candidates {v1}, {v2}, {v3} and {v4} can be removed from all other cells in {unit} (red), as they are exclusively reserved for those four cells.

xyWing.title: XY-Wing
xyWing.step1Desc: Pivot cell [R{pr}C{pc}] has candidates [{x},{y}]. Pincer 1 [R{r1}C{c1}] has [{x},{z}] and sees the pivot. Pincer 2 [R{r2}C{c2}] has [{y},{z}] and sees the pivot. Regardless of which candidate the pivot takes, one of the pincers must contain {z}.
xyWing.step2Desc: Every cell that sees both pincers (red) cannot contain {z} — because one of the pincers will always hold {z}. The elimination is guaranteed regardless of the pivot's solution.

xyzWing.title: XYZ-Wing
xyzWing.step1Desc: Pivot [R{pr}C{pc}] has three candidates [{x},{y},{z}]. Both pincers [R{r1}C{c1}] and [R{r2}C{c2}] see the pivot and contain {z} plus one of {x} or {y}. Unlike XY-Wing, the pivot itself also contains {z}, so the elimination only applies to cells that see ALL three members.
xyzWing.step2Label: Eliminate z from cells that see all three
xyzWing.step2Desc: Cells that see the pivot and both pincers (red) cannot contain {z} — in every scenario one of the three cells holds it. This is a more restrictive but more powerful version of XY-Wing.

sueDeCoq.title: Sue-de-Coq
sueDeCoq.step1Desc: The intersection of row {row} and box {box} contains cells whose candidates [{intDigits}] split into two groups: [{rowExclusive}] that can only go into the rest of the row, and [{boxExclusive}] that can only go into the rest of the box. The intersection (blue) "consumes" all those candidates.
sueDeCoq.step2Label: Eliminate from the row and box
sueDeCoq.step2Desc: Since [{rowExclusive}] are exclusive to the intersection within the row, they cannot appear in the rest of the row. Since [{boxExclusive}] are exclusive to the intersection within the box, they cannot appear in the rest of the box. Red cells lose those candidates.

xyChain.title: XY-Chain (length {len})
xyChain.step1Desc: Chain of bivalue cells: {chain}. Every two adjacent cells share one candidate and "see" each other. Regardless of the direction in which the chain resolves, digit {digit} must be at one of the two chain ends (blue).
xyChain.step2Desc: Cells that see both ends of the chain (red) can never have {digit} — one chain end always holds {digit}. This elimination can open the path to the solution.

bug.title: BUG+1 (Bivalue Universal Grave)
bug.step1Desc: Almost every empty cell has exactly two candidates — that is the "Bivalue Universal Grave" (BUG) state. The only exception is cell [R{row}C{col}] which has three candidates: [{cands}]. If we don't enter the correct digit here, the puzzle would deadlock with multiple solutions.
bug.step2Desc: The only candidate that breaks the BUG symmetry is {digit} — it appears an odd number of times in its row, column, or box. Entering {digit} resolves the BUG and guarantees solution uniqueness.

emptyRectangle.title: Empty Rectangle
emptyRectangle.step1Label: Spot the Empty Rectangle in the box
emptyRectangle.step1Desc: In box {box}, all candidates for {val} lie in row {erRow} (blue). This creates an "empty rectangle" — the rest of the box has no {val}. Column {col} has a strong link for {val}: only in rows {rowA} and {rowB}.
emptyRectangle.step2Desc: The strong link in column {col} and the box alignment together guarantee that {val} must be in row {erRow} or in the box column containing the ER. Cell [R{row}C{col2}] sees both — therefore {val} is eliminated from it.

wWing.title: W-Wing
wWing.step1Desc: Two cells [R{r1}C{c1}] and [R{r2}C{c2}] have the same candidates [{p},{q}] and cannot see each other. They are connected by a "strong link" on digit {p} — there is a unit where {p} can only go into two cells, one seeing the first bivalue cell and the other seeing the second. This guarantees at least one of the bivalue cells contains {q}.
wWing.step2Label: Eliminate q from cells that see both
wWing.step2Desc: Every cell that sees both [R{r1}C{c1}] and [R{r2}C{c2}] (red) cannot contain {q} — because one of the bivalue cells always holds {q}, without exception.

jellyfish.title: Jellyfish
jellyfish.step1Desc: Digit {val} appears in 2–4 places in each of four rows ({rows}), and all candidates fall within exactly 4 columns. This is a four-dimensional X-Wing (or two-dimensional Swordfish): {val} is "locked" inside that 4×4 grid (blue).
jellyfish.step2Desc: Regardless of the arrangement inside the matrix, {val} covers all four columns across those four rows. Therefore {val} cannot appear in any other cell of those columns (red).

uniqueRectangleType1.title: Unique Rectangle — Type 1
uniqueRectangleType1.step1Desc: Three cells of the rectangle have exactly [{a},{b}]. The fourth cell [R{row}C{col}] has additional candidates. If it also had only {a} and {b}, the puzzle would have multiple solutions — impossible in a valid sudoku.
uniqueRectangleType1.step2Label: Eliminate the pair from the fourth cell
uniqueRectangleType1.step2Desc: {a} and {b} are eliminated from [R{row}C{col}] to guarantee solution uniqueness. The remaining candidates determine the correct value.

uniqueRectangleType2.title: Unique Rectangle — Type 2
uniqueRectangleType2.step1Desc: Two cells of the rectangle are exactly [{a},{b}]. The other two are [{a},{b},{extra}] — the same extra candidate {extra}. If {extra} is not in one of those cells, the puzzle deadlocks with multiple solutions. Therefore {extra} must be in one of them.
uniqueRectangleType2.step2Label: Eliminate extra digit from shared peers
uniqueRectangleType2.step2Desc: Since {extra} must be in [R{row1}C{col1}] or [R{row2}C{col2}], any cell that sees both (red) cannot contain {extra}.

twoStringKite.title: Two-String Kite
twoStringKite.step1Desc: Digit {val} has exactly two candidates in row {row} and exactly two in column {col}. Both "kite strings" share cell [R{row}C{col}] inside the same 3×3 box. Regardless of whether {val} is in [R{row}C{rowEnd}] or [R{colEnd}C{col}] — one end always holds {val}.
twoStringKite.step2Label: Eliminate from cells that see both ends
twoStringKite.step2Desc: Cells that see both [R{row}C{rowEnd}] and [R{colEnd}C{col}] (red) cannot have {val} — one of the two kite ends always holds it. This is the equivalent of a Skyscraper but shaped like a kite instead.

skyscraper.title: Skyscraper
skyscraper.step1Desc: Digit {val} appears exactly twice in row {rowA} (cols {colA1} and {colA2}) and twice in row {rowB} (cols {colB1} and {colB2}). They share column {sharedCol} but in different boxes — like a skyscraper leaning on the same pillar. The tops (blue: C{topA} and C{topB}) must contain {val} in at least one of those two rows.
skyscraper.step2Label: Eliminate from cells that see both tops
skyscraper.step2Desc: Since {val} must occupy at least one of the two tops (C{topA} in row {rowA}, or C{topB} in row {rowB}), every cell that sees both tops (red) cannot have {val} — one of the tops always holds it.

swordfish.title: Swordfish
swordfish.step1Desc: Digit {val} appears in 2–3 places in each of rows {row1}, {row2} and {row3}, and all candidates fall within exactly 3 columns. Like a three-dimensional X-Wing: {val} must be in one of the cells of that 3×3 grid (blue).
swordfish.step2Desc: Regardless of the arrangement inside the matrix, {val} covers all three columns across those three rows. Therefore {val} cannot appear in any other cell of those columns (red) — the elimination applies to entire columns.

boxLineReductionRow.title: Box-Line Reduction — Row
boxLineReductionRow.step1Label: Digit locked to one row inside the box
boxLineReductionRow.step1Desc: In row {row}, digit {val} only appears in cells that all lie within the same 3×3 box (blue). This means {val} must go into one of those cells — exclusively within that row inside the box.
boxLineReductionRow.step2Label: Eliminate from the rest of the box
boxLineReductionRow.step2Desc: Since {val} must be in row {row} inside that box, it cannot appear in any other cell of that same box (red). This elimination is called Box-Line Reduction — the complement of Pointing Pair.

boxLineReductionColumn.title: Box-Line Reduction — Column
boxLineReductionColumn.step1Label: Digit locked to one column inside the box
boxLineReductionColumn.step1Desc: In column {col}, digit {val} only appears in cells that all lie within the same 3×3 box (blue). {val} must go into one of those cells — exclusively within that column inside the box.
boxLineReductionColumn.step2Label: Eliminate from the rest of the box
boxLineReductionColumn.step2Desc: Since {val} must be in column {col} inside that box, it cannot appear in any other cell of that box (red). Box-Line Reduction is the complement of the Pointing Pair technique.

hiddenQuad.title: Hidden Quad — {unit}
hiddenQuad.step1Desc: Digits {digits} can only go into four cells within {unit}. These cells have other candidates, but {digits} are exclusive to this quad — they cannot go anywhere else in the unit.
hiddenQuad.step2Label: Eliminate other candidates from the quad
hiddenQuad.step2Desc: Since {digits} must fill exactly those four cells, all other candidates within them are impossible and are removed (red). This converts the hidden quad into a naked quad.

hiddenTriple.title: Hidden Triple — {unit}
hiddenTriple.step1Desc: Digits {v1}, {v2} and {v3} can only go into cells [R{row1}C{col1}], [R{row2}C{col2}] and [R{row3}C{col3}] within {unit}. This triple is "hidden" — the cells have other candidates, but {v1}, {v2}, {v3} are exclusive to these three cells.
hiddenTriple.step2Label: Eliminate other candidates from the triple
hiddenTriple.step2Desc: Since {v1}, {v2} and {v3} must fill exactly those three cells, all other candidates in them are impossible (red). Eliminating them reveals new naked singles or pairs.

hiddenPair.title: Hidden Pair
hiddenPair.step1Desc: Digits {v1} and {v2} can only go into cells [R{row1}C{col1}] and [R{row2}C{col2}] within this unit. Even if those cells have other candidates, {v1} and {v2} are "hidden" inside them — they cannot go anywhere else in the unit.
hiddenPair.step2Label: Eliminate other candidates from the pair
hiddenPair.step2Desc: Since {v1} and {v2} must occupy exactly those two cells, all other candidates in them are impossible and can be eliminated (red). This effectively converts the hidden pair into a naked pair.

xWing.title: X-Wing
xWing.step1Desc: Digit {val} appears exactly twice in row {row1} (columns {col1} and {col2}) and exactly twice in row {row2} (the same columns). These four cells form a rectangle — the basis of the X-Wing technique. {val} must lie on one of the two diagonals of that rectangle.
xWing.step2Desc: Regardless of which diagonal is correct, {val} must be in columns {col1} and {col2} within those two rows. Therefore {val} cannot appear anywhere else in those columns. Red cells can have {val} removed as a candidate.

candidateReduction.title: Candidate Reduction (Analysis)
candidateReduction.step1Desc: Cell [R{row}C{col}] has the fewest remaining possibilities. Blue cells in its row and column block the wrong candidates, leaving only {num} as the only mathematically valid option. Try applying the earlier techniques for better understanding.
candidateReduction.step2Desc: After eliminating the wrong options, {num} is the only value that does not violate any sudoku rule for this cell. Click "Apply Answer" to confirm.

modal.winHintMsg: You solved the puzzle with the analyzer in {time}.
modal.winInstantMsg: You solved the puzzle with the analyzer!

customImport.title: Custom Puzzle
customImport.description: Paste 81 digits (0 = empty cell). Spaces and newlines are ignored.
customImport.errorBoxDuplicate: Box at row {row} col {col} has duplicate {value}.
customImport.errorNoSolution: This puzzle has no solution — check the digits and try again.
customImport.errorMultipleSolutions: This puzzle has more than one solution, so mistakes could not be checked fairly. Add more givens.
